column2: Defining exactly what the project team must do and create to produce the final product.
Developing a detailed document that explains how the project will be executed to meet all requirements.
Using the project plan to organize and guide the team's daily work toward meeting targets.
Learning exactly what the customer wants the final product to do to solve their problem.
Translating user needs into detailed technical instructions that tell builders what the product must be and do.
Providing the detailed technical specifications to the project team so they know what to produce.
Setting up the team structure and holding a kickoff meeting to start the project.
The first formal meeting where the manager explains goals and builds team commitment.
Working closely with the customer to turn vague ideas into a clear and detailed list of needs.
Meeting with the client to get their official approval on the requirements and the project plan.
The high-level "whats" that the customer wants the project to provide.
Specific technical requirements that describe the functions the system must perform.
The specific levels or measurable targets the system must reach to be successful.
Recognizing that customers are often unclear about what they need and helping them define it.
Making system definition a team effort by involving users and stakeholders in the planning.
Using an iterative process to update details as the project plan expands and changes.
Technical instructions written by experts to guide the actual design and build work.
Getting both the user and the project staff to officially agree to and sign the requirements.
Using pictures and models to help define requirements so they aren't misinterpreted.
Keeping track of how changes to one requirement affect others to ensure goals are still met.
Assigning importance to requirements and defining how much they are allowed to vary.
A document that defines the boundaries of the project and exactly what work is included.
Breaking down the total project work into smaller
Identifying which team members are responsible for completing specific tasks.
A timeline showing when specific tasks will happen and when key goals must be reached.
The financial plan for the project combined with a plan for handling potential problems.
A guide that describes who needs what information and how meetings will be handled.
A "Waterfall" approach where each stage of the project must be finished before the next begins.
An approach used when requirements are very clear from the start and unlikely to change.
An approach where the project is developed in small cycles or increments rather than all at once.
Breaking the end product into pieces so the customer gets working parts of the system early.
Using agile or iterative steps when the final product is hard to visualize or conceptualize.
Starting with a rough plan and adding more detail as the project moves forward and unknowns decrease.
Allowing the user to test early versions (prototypes) to help finalize the requirements.
A document issued by senior management to officially start a project and give the manager power.
Setting the high-level goals and limits for the project within the charter.
Identifying potential threats and the rules or limits the project must follow.
Identifying the people who have a stake in the project and what their needs are.
